chunk_summary: Physiology-based feeding decisions in congenital heart disease (CHD) focus on safety, aspiration risk, and delivery efficiency, with tailored pathways for feeding intolerance management.
learning_objectives: Understand physiology-based feeding intolerance in CHD, including pulmonary overcirculation, systemic hypoperfusion, and global hypoxia.
Identify unsafe feeding signs such as rising lactate, worsening perfusion, or abdominal pathology.
Learn practical feeding pathways, including human milk use, fortification, tube feeding, and parenteral nutrition (PN).
key_facts: CHD feeding intolerance is categorized into pulmonary overcirculation, systemic hypoperfusion, and global hypoxia.
Unsafe feeding signs include rising lactate, worsening perfusion, and escalating vasoactive support.
Feeding inefficiency due to tachypnea and fatigue responds to calorie concentration and tube assistance.
Human milk is preferred; fortify or concentrate when fluids are restricted.
Post-pyloric feeds are recommended for reflux or aspiration risk; PN is added when enteral feeding fails.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: In CHD, a physiology-based approach helps distinguish unsafe feeding from inefficient feeding. (Pediatric Cardiac Intensive Care Society, Nutrition guidelines for the pediatric and neonatal congenital heart patient)
document_key_topics: Safety and aspiration risk in feeding
Feeding pathways: human milk, fortification, tube feeding, PN
